bins.name: Support ANOPR Goals
bins.desc: EEI endorses the ANOPR's stated goals of improving the speed, efficiency, fairness, and predictability of large load interconnections, framing its comments as constructive enhancements rather than opposition.
quotes: EEI shares the Administration's commitment to American energy dominance and specifically supports the ANOPR's goals to improve the speed, efficiency, fairness, and predictability of large load interconnections.

bins.name: Raise Default Threshold to 100 MW
bins.desc: EEI opposes the 20 MW default threshold as too low and too broad, arguing it would capture traditional industrial customers outside the ANOPR's intent. EEI proposes a minimum national default of 100 MW while allowing regional variation downward based on local system characteristics.
quotes: the 20 MW threshold is inappropriate for the specific, high-impact load customers that the ANOPR is intending to address. If the Commission adopts a universal default or threshold definition of large load customers premised on that customer's (gross) peak demand, it should be no lower than 100 MW, allowing for flexibility to reflect the unique circumstances of the region.

the potential clarity and ease of a universal threshold for large load would likely be outweighed by over- or under-inclusion of impactful large load with corresponding high costs for inadequate improvement to [bulk power system] reliability.

bins.name: Rolled-In Rather Than Direct Assignment
bins.desc: EEI opposes 100% direct assignment of network upgrade costs to large loads, arguing it conflicts with decades of FERC precedent treating network upgrades as system-wide assets recoverable through rolled-in rates. Large loads should instead pay their proportional share through transmission rates.
quotes: Rather than only directly assigning costs of the network upgrades necessary to connect large load customers, the Commission should require them to pay for their share of the transmission system.

The Commission's longstanding precedent is that network upgrade costs are rolled into transmission providers' rates and charged to all system customers. As a result, all customers pay for their share of the transmission system based on their connection to and use of the system and thus, large load customers will, by virtue of their size, pay for their proportional share of the costs.

bins.name: Cost Allocation Protects All Customers
bins.desc: EEI supports a cost allocation framework that spreads network upgrade costs through rolled-in rates while using financial commitments from large load customers to protect other ratepayers if a project does not materialize.
quotes: Rather than only directly assigning costs of the network upgrades necessary to connect large load customers, the Commission should require them to pay for their share of the transmission system.

The Commission's longstanding precedent is that network upgrade costs are rolled into transmission providers' rates and charged to all system customers. As a result, all customers pay for their share of the transmission system based on their connection to and use of the system and thus, large load customers will, by virtue of their size, pay for their proportional share of the costs.

bins.name: Strong Deposits Deter Speculation
bins.desc: EEI supports study deposits sized to both cover study costs and discourage speculative queue entries, drawing on the lesson from generator interconnection queues that became unmanageable when readiness requirements were weak.
quotes: EEI member companies agree that any large-load interconnection program or tariff adopted by the Commission must address speculative projects and provide a framework to allow transmission providers to more accurately forecast demand on their systems. With a large load interconnection, a study will need to be performed. Study deposits sufficient not only to cover the cost of the study but also to deter speculative study requests are appropriate.

Generator interconnection queues became unmanageable precisely because they permitted entry without strong readiness requirements which hindered transmission planning and delayed needed infrastructure.

bins.name: Flexible but Anti-Speculative Study Process
bins.desc: EEI supports interconnection study processes that are flexible enough to handle diverse load characteristics but include strong readiness requirements and adequate deposits to prevent the queue saturation seen in generator interconnection programs.
quotes: EEI member companies agree that any large-load interconnection program or tariff adopted by the Commission must address speculative projects and provide a framework to allow transmission providers to more accurately forecast demand on their systems. With a large load interconnection, a study will need to be performed. Study deposits sufficient not only to cover the cost of the study but also to deter speculative study requests are appropriate.

Generator interconnection queues became unmanageable precisely because they permitted entry without strong readiness requirements which hindered transmission planning and delayed needed infrastructure.

bins.name: Gross Load Default for Co-Located Facilities
bins.desc: EEI supports requiring co-located loads to pay for transmission and ancillary services on a gross basis as the default, consistent with FERC precedent under Order No. 888. Generation diversion to serve co-located large load should also trigger a reliability study before proceeding.
quotes: to ensure fairness and avoid implementation complexity, the Commission should make a gross load approach the default for both ancillary services and transmission, subject to exception only if a utility supports and the state approves a different approach.
The Commission should not adopt a default rule that could disguise some large load customers' level of reliance on the transmission system.
EEI strongly supports requiring that any planned diversion of existing generation be studied for transmission reliability prior to it becoming dedicated to serving a retail large load.

bins.name: No Free-Riding on Transmission Grid
bins.desc: EEI supports gross-load cost allocation for co-located facilities to prevent large loads from disguising their transmission dependence through netting arrangements that would shift costs to other customers.
quotes: to ensure fairness and avoid implementation complexity, the Commission should make a gross load approach the default for both ancillary services and transmission, subject to exception only if a utility supports and the state approves a different approach.
The Commission should not adopt a default rule that could disguise some large load customers' level of reliance on the transmission system.

bins.name: Reliability Safeguards for Option to Build
bins.desc: EEI does not flatly oppose an option to build for large loads but argues it requires strict safeguards: limiting scope to interconnection facilities and stand-alone upgrades, requiring transmission owner approval of engineering and construction, and mandating indemnification. Generation diversion must also be studied for reliability and resource adequacy impacts.
quotes: the potential clarity and ease of a universal threshold for large load would likely be outweighed by over- or under-inclusion of impactful large load with corresponding high costs for inadequate improvement to [bulk power system] reliability.
providing large load customers with the option to build such OTB facilities raises significant reliability concerns that must be addressed.

if the Commission requires an option to build be provided to all large load customers, it must also require necessary safeguards to protect the reliability of the transmission grid. First, it must ensure that the option to build is strictly limited to transmission owner interconnection facilities and stand-alone network upgrades, to limit any broader system impacts.
EEI strongly supports requiring that any planned diversion of existing generation be studied for transmission reliability prior to it becoming dedicated to serving a retail large load.

bins.name: State Jurisdiction Over Retail Load Interconnection
bins.desc: EEI argues that retail load interconnection has been regulated by states under the existing FPA framework and that FERC has not previously exercised jurisdiction in this space. EEI warns that asserting jurisdiction could raise major questions doctrine concerns and urges the Commission to advance reforms on sound jurisdictional footing.
quotes: interconnection service for retail end-use customers (including large load customers) is regulated by the states under the existing regulatory paradigm. Any Commission action must recognize that large load customers are end-use retail customers, meaning the delivery service they receive necessarily includes an element of local distribution service.

Reading the statute to take away authority from states that they have consistently applied, without a clear Congressional mandate to do so, may run afoul of the major questions doctrine and would be something the Commission must consider.

bins.name: Preserve State and RTO Progress
bins.desc: EEI urges FERC to avoid disrupting existing state tariffs, bilateral agreements, and RTO processes already in place for large load interconnection. A final rule should accommodate and complement these arrangements rather than override them.
quotes: The Commission should ensure that it does not disrupt what states, utilities, and large load customers already have achieved to bring online large loads.
the Commission should ensure that any national procedures do not undermine the RTO and utility-specific processes.

bins.name: Extend Expedited Study to Proximate Loads
bins.desc: EEI supports extending any accelerated study benefits to load-generation arrangements that are electrically proximate but not at the same point of interconnection, citing the SPP HILL/HILLGA model as an example.
quotes: the Commission should extend accelerated study benefits—if any are established in a Final Rule—to arrangements involving load and facilities located in proximity to each other but not at the same point of interconnection.

bins.name: Proximity as Interconnection Criterion
bins.desc: EEI endorses using electrical proximity as a criterion for accelerated study benefits, consistent with the SPP approach that links load-limited generation resources to nearby high-impact large load customers.
quotes: the Commission should extend accelerated study benefits—if any are established in a Final Rule—to arrangements involving load and facilities located in proximity to each other but not at the same point of interconnection.

bins.name: SPP HILL Process as Model
bins.desc: EEI cites the SPP High Impact Large Load process and related HILLGA generation assessment as an illustration of how large load interconnection can be addressed within existing jurisdictional limits, without endorsing the specifics of that proposal.
quotes: the Commission should ensure that any national procedures do not undermine the RTO and utility-specific processes.

bins.name: PJM Stakeholder Process Ongoing
bins.desc: EEI notes that PJM has an ongoing stakeholder process producing promising proposals for large load interconnection and urges that a final federal rule not undermine those regional efforts.
quotes: the Commission should ensure that any national procedures do not undermine the RTO and utility-specific processes.
